reply_text:
Hi @! I wouldn't recommend [POLICY], as you have a [POLICY], and probably don't meet the [POLICY] we require of people to have stand alone articles. However, if you have other subjects here's the steps to take:
# Ask yourself whether they meet [POLICY] (primarily, do they have significant coverage in [POLICY], independent, [POLICY]?)
# if the subject does, I would begin by creating a draft through [POLICY]. If you navigate to that link, there will be a blue button that says "Click here to start a new article". Click it! And you can create a draft page start writing your article, and submit it for review when it is 'done'.
# Make sure the article is cited to reliable sources, written from a [POLICY], and [POLICY].
For help with this, I would recommend reading [HELP_PAGE], and looking at existing articles on similar topics to see how they are written. [POLICY] is an interactive guide to the *basics* of editing, and if you have any further questions, feel free to ask them here or at [POLICY]! There are a lot of friendly editors happy to help you out. You can also experiment with editing on the draft you create (in step 2) without any major repercussions, or at [POLICY]. One of the best ways to learn how to edit is by doing it! We're here to help you through the inevitable mistakes
Happy editing,

question_text: does wikipedia have any rules about self-consistency or anything? this article ([LINK] states SMS Pommern was "the fastest pre-dreadnought battleship in the world" and describes its speed as 19 knots but this article ([LINK] states the Regina Margherita had a speed of 20 knots. It's so glaringly obvious that 20 knots is faster than 19 knots that I'm tempted to edit it, but I really want to be careful because I'm new to Wikipedia.
reply_text: The issue isn't internal consistency, it's just probably what the work being referenced says. The authors of "Die deutschen Kriegsschiffe" likely weren't aware of the Regina Margherita when they made that statement. Rather than removing it, the statement could be attributed (the Pommern was described as the fastest pre-dreadnought battleship in the world), but if you should make the direct comparison is a better question for the military history editors. Try asking on the talk page? [LINK]

question_text: How do I edit?
reply_text:
Hi @, welcome to Wikipedia! You can edit [POLICY] every page on Wikipedia by clicking the "Edit" button. There are some policies and guidelines about content and editing practices; you'll learn those as you start editing. Generally speaking, if there's something you can do to improve Wikipedia, be [POLICY] and do it! If you mess up, anyone can easily fix any mistakes you might have made. However, I would recommend that you read about the most important principles of Wikipedia before you start editing: the [POLICY]. Another great resource to get started is [POLICY]. You should also be aware that editors are discouraged from writing about themselves or topics that they have a connection to (like employers or friends), as that makes it [POLICY] to be [POLICY]. Last thing, I promise: Everything in Wikipedia articles should be based on [POLICY] so that readers can [POLICY] it.
I know it can feel overwhelming to have all this information to read through, but don't be discouraged; you'll learn the ropes in no time, and the best way to do that is to just start improving articles in whatever way you can. If you have any questions, I'd be happy to help you. You can reply to this message and I should get back to you quite quickly :) Happy editing! ,

Hi , welcome to Wikipedia!
Creating an article from scratch is very difficult, and it may be a frustrating experience because you need to be familiar with a lot of different policies & guidelines on how to write appropriate content for Wikipedia. It‘s not usually recommended for new editors, but it‘s entirely allowed. [POLICY] provides guidance for new users on writing an article, including some of the more technical aspects. I suggest you read through that, as well as some of the policies it links to. I‘d be happy to help you with any questions.
There is a basic requirement for standalone article topics called [POLICY]. In most cases, that means that independent and reliable sources need to have published significant content on the subject.
Something else to be aware of is that it's generally not a good idea to create articles about yourself, your family or friends, or a company or organization that you work for or with. That's mainly because it's extremely difficult to write from a [POLICY] in such cases. You can read more about conflicts of interest [POLICY].
I know it can be discouraging to have all these guidelines thrown at you, but please don‘t let it dissuade you from editing here on Wikipedia. Meanwhile, it may be good to go around and improve existing articles to learn the ropes. If you see something wrong, don‘t worry too much about knowing all the guidelines; be [POLICY] and fix it! Happy editing! :) ,

question_text: Hello, when can I start writing a new article?
reply_text:
, to answer your question, you can start writing a new article whenever you want.
However, note that you can't create an article directly in 'mainspace', or where viewers see it. Instead, there is a technical restriction which only allows you to create a new article directly when you are [POLICY] - that is, your account has existed for four complete days and made ten edits.
Furthermore, creating a new article is one of the most difficult things you could possibly do on Wikipedia, only made even more arduous since you are a new editor. This would be akin to attending a chess competition having just learned what the rules are.
If your mind is set on creating a new article, I highly recommend you spend some time (at least a few days) making edits suggested at , then reading [POLICY] very carefully and creating a draft. Also note that creating articles is generally not the best way you can contribute to Wikipedia.
Lastly, if you are being [POLICY] to edit Wikipedia or have a [POLICY] with your planned article subject, then you should read the relevant guideline/policy, which are linked earlier in the sentence. Happy editing!

question_text:
Hi! What is the protocol for links within an article to other wikipedia pages that are only loosely relevant to the originating article (which I see on occasion).
Purely as an example, let's say there's a page about a fashion model and within that article there is this sentence; "Jane Smith began her modeling career when she was discovered by a Ford Modeling Agency executive on her way to the grocery store to buy bread." There might be interlinks to wikipedia pages for "grocery store" and "bread". These links don't appear to be necessary or really add anything of value since what is being linked to is only nominally relevant to the article where the links originate from and virtually everyone knows what a grocery store is and what bread is.
Alternately, let's say you have an article about corner stores or bodegas, which still exist and were the precursors to grocery stores. In an article like that, you would expect to see "grocery store" linked to if the text appeared somewhere in the article because it's highly relevant to the content on the page where the link is originating from. Linking to "grocery store" in the context of a page about bodegas (via which readers can find out how they're related, the differences, etc.), makes perfect sense here where in the article about a model, linking to "grocery store" serves no real purpose.
Are there any rules to this or preferred protocol? Thanks.
reply_text: @ My thoughts are that words like "grocery store" and "bread" should be known to the target reader of Wikipedia, and it's also tangentially related to Janes modeling career, so it doesn't make sense to link it. In your second example, linking to "grocery store" makes sense, if barely so, since it's more related to the topic at hand.

question_text: Hello, I’m new to Wikipedia and interested in creating a biography article about a notable entrepreneur who has been performing exceptionally well in the business world. I noticed their photo is available on Wikimedia Commons and there’s a Wikidata profile as well. Could you please guide me on how to start a draft for a new article and what sources I should use to meet Wikipedia’s notability criteria?
reply_text: Alex261995, the page at [HELP_PAGE] breaks down what you need to know. The main point is that you need a few reliable sources that are independent of the subject. Press coverage from major publishers is good, but things like interviews or company websites should be used sparingly and don't count toward notability. The other main thing to keep in mind is the [POLICY]. Make sure the article doesn't sound like it's celebrating the person or highlighting their successes. Wikipedia is especially strict about this on people related to business because there are many who misuse Wikipedia as a way to promote themselves. There are also [POLICY] if you know the person or are connected to them in some way. If you have the necessary sources and there's enough to write a neutral description of the person, then you can start at the [POLICY]. You're also welcome to practice editing by fixing problems on already existing articles, or to ask questions at [POLICY], which is a page specifically for new editors.

@: Hi and welcome to Wikipedia! Hopefully my answer will encompass everything you should have taken care of – but sadly I don't see the text (you probably haven't uploaded it yet) so my judgment and advices will be perhaps quite general.
The main concern you should have as an editor is being connected to the subject (the company). This is regulated by [POLICY] policy which states you should disclose all connections (furthermore, if you're being paid to create this article please see [POLICY] as well). Next question, which is probably one of the most important steps when creating an article is proving it is notable. Every subject must pass the [POLICY] criteria and (in your case) [POLICY] policy as well – and you prove that by having enough in-depth independent [POLICY] on the subject (see also [POLICY] on how to format them). I know I have provided you lots of policies etc. but rest assured article creation is one of the hardest tasks a newcomer could undertake.
Since you have conflict of interest, you'll need to publish a draft through [POLICY], where in case your content wouldn't be compliant with Wikipedia policies, you'd get feedback on how to improve it. Best case, your draft gets accepted.
I think I gave you the outline of everything you'd need to be concerned about, however if you have any further questions please do not hesitate to ask. Best regards, '|

question_text:
Hello,
I would like to start with creating a page on myself. I am a mathematician and would like to list my math equations and conjectures that I have worked on. What advice would you have for me starting out, like the top 5+ things to look out for and not do.
reply_text:
Hello @! Thank you for reaching out. I'm no expert on math content on Wikipedia, but here are some things to keep in mind:
# Articles on Wikipedia must meet the [LINK]. To summarize: There must be reliable sources (articles, books) that treat the topic non-trivially. Additionally, these sources must be reliable (reputable), secondary, and must have been produced by people not too closely affiliated with the subject. Getting this level of coverage on an equation or conjecture may prove challenging.
# You may need to disclose a conflict of interest if, say, you "discovered" or first formulated the math you want to write about. See [POLICY] for a more thorough treatment of this topic.
# If you believe what you want to write about meets these criteria, I would recommend looking at how other math articles are written on Wikipedia (e.g. [WIKILINK]) and trying to improve them before starting on your own article. This will help you gain a basic familiarity with the conventions on Wikipedia.
# When you're ready, read the page [HELP_PAGE].
Hopefully this is helpful. I would suggest reaching out with more topic-specific questions in [POLICY]. That's a community of Wikipedians who specialize in editing about math. Cheers!

question_text: Hello i am an artist from india wanted to create my wikipedia page
reply_text:
As you may already know, Wikipedia is a collaborative encyclopedia written by its readers. The subjects for our articles are generally chosen by the editing community rather than by request. While there is a venue where you can request articles, a request for an article creation on oneself is likely to be declined. You can visit our 'requests for articles' page at .
It's also important to realise that Wikipedia strongly discourages articles written by the subjects themselves or by others close to a subject because of the difficulty in writing objectively about yourself, your family, or your work. Please see for our guidelines on writing about yourself or topics you are personally involved with. While keeping in mind conflict of interest issues, please note that if someone does create an article about you, you should feel more than welcome to edit the article to ensure that each piece of information is factual and complying with our "Biography of Living Persons" policy. This policy can be viewed at .
You may also like to read for a guideline on the type of articles that are generally accepted.
Thank you for your interest in Wikipedia and feel free to get back in touch with us with any further comments or questions.

question_text: what can i add articles on
reply_text: Mike899 welcome to Wikipedia and thanks for the question! Wikipedia hosts articles on topics that are "[POLICY]"—i.e. that have received in-depth coverage in multiple reliable, independent sources. However, creating a Wikipedia article is very difficult because it requires experience in lots of different skills. The most important stage is the research stage, where you need to find sources and [POLICY]. If you decide your topic is definitely notable then you need to summarise the sources in your own words, making sure the reader can [POLICY]. Then, you need good writing skills and the ability to format using [POLICY] so the article is readable. Newcomers should start with tasks that help them develop these skills one at a time, some of which are recommended on . The ideal is to start by adding reliable sources to existing articles that are in poor shape. —

question_text: Hi would you be able to check my draft?
reply_text:
Thickboy123 Good day. First of all you need to provide the URL of the page or the name of the draft page so the person who you seek help from would know the issue at hand. All the sources in your draft [DRAFT] fails GNG requirements.
In order for a subject to warrant a page in the main space of Wikipedia, it must fulfill the criteria outlined in the Wikipedia guideline on notability, specifically the General Notability Guideline ([POLICY]).
GNG is defined as the subject must fulfill specific criteria to demonstrate [POLICY] from [POLICY] and [POLICY] sources. These sources should provide in-depth discussions about the subject rather than merely offering passing mentions. This requirement is essential for verification, which is a core policy of Wikipedia. If a source is affiliated/associated/connected with the subject—such as the subject's marketing team, company, social media platforms, information in interviews of the subject or any self-published information—this indicates a lack of independence. Consequently, such sources cannot be utilized to satisfy the notability guidelines. Sources originating from educational institutions, government entities, foundations, social media platforms, and private companies are regarded as unreliable. In contrast, materials published by "major" newspapers and "reputable" books are typically considered reliable sources.
I recommend that you read [POLICY] and adhere to the provided instructions before beginning the creation of an article.

In plainer terms (while I can't actually see the contents of a removed article) this usually happens when the article looks similar to what you would find in a resume or CV. CVs are problematic for Wikipedia for multiple reasons, for example, they are also typically not sourced to the types of sources articles need to be written from, for example, sources need to be both independent and secondary (see [POLICY]). For more detail, [POLICY] and [POLICY] offer guidance on these situations (the first one covers more than just autobiographies). ( • )

question_text: Hello! How can I edit a biography page about someone I know personally?
reply_text: Hello Hannah.keen22, and welcome to Wikipedia! You should start by reading our [POLICY]. The hardest part about writing on Wikipedia about someone you know is that you can only add information to the article if it has been published somewhere that meets [POLICY]-- so there's likely to be a lot you know that has to be left out, to ensure that the encyclopedia is [POLICY]. If you let me know what specific changes you're hoping to make, I may be able to help make them. I'm also happy to answer any other questions you may have.

question_text: Bonsoir j'espère que vous vous portez bien ? Puis-je savoir comment faire une biographie ?
reply_text:
OLLOMO ABAGA Nathaniel Good day.
In order for a subject to warrant a page in the main space of Wikipedia, it must fulfill the criteria outlined in the Wikipedia guideline on notability, specifically the General Notability Guideline ([POLICY]). Since this is English Wikipedia, the content of the article needs to be in English.
GNG is defined as the subject must fulfill specific criteria to demonstrate [POLICY] from [POLICY] and [POLICY] sources. These sources should provide in-depth discussions about the subject rather than merely offering passing mentions. This requirement is essential for verification, which is a core policy of Wikipedia. If a source is affiliated/associated/connected with the subject—such as the subject's marketing team, company, social media platforms, information in interviews of the subject or any self-published information—this indicates a lack of independence. Consequently, such sources cannot be utilized to satisfy the notability guidelines. Sources originating from educational institutions, government entities, foundations, social media platforms, and private companies are regarded as unreliable. In contrast, materials published by "major" newspapers and "reputable" books are typically considered reliable sources.
You can use the "horizontal format" of [TEMPLATE_REF] if the source is from the web for inline citation. I recommend that you read [POLICY] and adhere to the provided instructions before beginning the creation of an article. Regards.

question_text: I would like to edit the NSMV Wikipedia page ([LINK] The page does not contain the complete story surrounding the NSMV, has factual errors, and is out of date. My question is - how substantially can an existing page be edited? What happens if a page undergoes substantial changes/edits? Thanks for the help/advice!
reply_text: IWasInTheRoom It is usually best to break up large edits into multiple smaller ones. This makes it less likely that there is an [HELP_PAGE] and can be easier to actually implement the edits. Also, remember that all information added to an article has to be sourced from a [POLICY]. If you're ever unsure about something in the page, you can always ask about it on the talk page. If you have any other questions, you can ask me here or ask over at the [POLICY].

question_text: Hi. If I want to suggest edits to an existing page, the CarMax page. I plan to self-identify that I am currently employed by the organization. What's the best way to request edits without risking a conflict of interest?
reply_text:
Hello, and welcome! That is a very good question. I am very glad to see someone ask that!
[POLICY] explains it all; when it comes to requesting edits, you can discuss the issue on the article's talk page. When viewing any article, there would be a "talk" tab below the article's title if you edit from a computer. Clicking that would pull up the talk page. There, you can request specific changes. Better yet, you can write the information you want the article to state (while being balanced and cited) for other people to help review and bring onto the article. [WIKILINK] has an example of what this looks like. If no one else replies to you, there is also an automatic edit request system, but I am not that sure of how it works.
:Does this help?

question_text: Good morning, I’m a judge with the King County Superior Court. I sit on the Court’s History Committee. We decided we would like to update our Wikipedia page, and I have been tasked with the job. There is a partial block in place on the page, preventing me from editing it. Any advice on how to get the block lifted?
reply_text:
KingCountyCourt Good evening. The short answer is, unfortunately, that we'd really prefer you don't do this kind of editing.
As a judge, you'll understand all about [POLICY], and I can assume you can see why it would be poor practice for us to allow every person to edit pages they have a COI with. At best, all our articles would end up full of—not to put too fine a point on it—promotional crap. At worst, we'd lose the public trust.
Now, we can't ban editing pages you have a conflict of interest with, but we really strongly discourage it. Wikipedia editors usually recuse themselves from editing topics they have a COI with. I linked our guideline on COI editing above, and you can find it at [POLICY].
There also might a second problem with the editing you're proposing. If you're being paid (or otherwise incentivized) for your edits, that's another kind of editing we really want to avoid dealing with. You can find our guidelines on that at [POLICY], but the bottom line there is that you must disclose any compensation you receive for your edits on . We take transparency very seriously.
There's also the more minor problem that your username is "KingCountyCourt", and our [POLICY] ask that you represent yourself as a person: don't use the name of your organization or any other formulation that implies shared use. See [POLICY] for examples and details on this. I'll post some links to your talk page explaining what you can do to fix this.
Wikipedia loves guidelines and rules, and I'm sorry you've ran into so many of them on your first day here! Don't panic about falling afoul of them—we're not going to ban you from editing because you're new and aren't sure about the huge number of rules. But the long story short is that we'd really, really prefer you don't edit like you're suggesting. I recommend you read [POLICY], which gives guidance for people in your position. (The general guideline there is: tell your boss it can't be done. Really.)
In response to the question you actually asked—sorry it took so long to get here—I presume you're asking about the article [WIKILINK]. That page currently has no protection on it, and according to the logs never has, so you may be tripping an edit filter or something else more complicated. If possible, can you link to a screenshot of what it looks like when you try and edit the page? Thanks! ( · )

question_text: Why I am not able to edit protected articles? What should I do to edit them?
reply_text:
Ramencolls Why I am not able to edit protected articles? What should I do to edit them?
Good day. You can only edit semi-protected pages if your account is at least 4 days old and has made at least 10 edits in the English Wikipedia main space. Both criteria must be met. For pages under extended confirmed protection, an account must be at least 30 days old and have made 500 edits in the English Wikipedia main space. Both criteria must be met. A fully protected page cannot be edited or moved by anyone except administrators. Let me know if you have further questions for I am here to help.
